pathophysiology_summary: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (amoxicillin, completed ~2 weeks earlier) suppress the protective vaginal lactobacilli, raising vaginal pH stability and permitting overgrowth of commensal Candida (usually C. albicans). Candidal hyphae invade the superficial vaginal and vulvar epithelium, provoking an inflammatory response that produces intense pruritus, vulvar erythema and oedema, a thick adherent white 'cottage-cheese' discharge composed of desquamated epithelium and pseudohyphae, external (splash) dysuria and superficial dyspareunia. The pre-menstrual flare reflects the known hormonal influence on candidal colonisation. It is a clinical diagnosis; a wet mount showing budding yeast and pseudohyphae (and normal vaginal pH ~4.0-4.5) confirms it but is not required to treat an uncomplicated episode. This is a benign, self-limiting, telehealth/pharmacist-manageable condition — the appropriate response is empirical antifungal therapy plus safety-netting, not urgent in-person escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Intense vulvar itch/burn with a thick white clumpy discharge is the classic picture; recent antibiotics are a strong precipitant.
Common cause of abnormal discharge — but typically a thin grey homogeneous discharge with a fishy odour and minimal itch, so must be actively excluded.
A sexually transmitted cause of vaginitis that can itch and burn; usually a frothy green-yellow malodorous discharge. Should be considered and, if risk factors or atypical features, tested for.
Scented products/soaps can cause vulvar irritation with itch and burning, but do not produce a curd-like discharge.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even at this stage vulvovaginal candidiasis should lead, with bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis retained until odour and discharge character are clarified. The AI Doctor should NOT reach for antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Curd-like discharge + intense itch + external dysuria/dyspareunia + recent antibiotics + absence of a fishy odour makes candidiasis the working diagnosis.
No fishy odour and a thick clumpy (not thin grey) discharge argue strongly against bacterial vaginosis.
Lower probability given the discharge character and lack of frothy malodorous discharge, but cannot be fully excluded without testing; keep on the list if it fails to respond.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Candidiasis should now be clearly leading; bacterial vaginosis effectively excluded by the negative odour and curd-like discharge. Empirical antifungal treatment is appropriate.

differential.evidence_basis: Characteristic uncomplicated episode precipitated by antibiotics — treat empirically with an antifungal and safety-net; swab only if it does not resolve, is recurrent, or is atypical.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. Definitive confirmation (wet mount / vaginal pH) is not required for an uncomplicated episode and cannot be obtained remotely — empirical treatment is the correct action.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors, or severe lower abdominal / pelvic pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would suggest ascending infection / pelvic inflammatory disease rather than simple vulvovaginitis, changing the tier and mandating urgent in-person assessment. Screened negative here (afebrile, systemically well, no pelvic pain).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed PID would allow ascending infection with risk to fertility; this is why fever/pelvic pain must be actively screened even in an apparently simple presentation.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would shift the diagnosis toward bacterial vaginosis or a sexually transmitted infection, requiring different (antibiotic) treatment and possibly STI testing. Absent here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Treating bacterial vaginosis or trichomoniasis with an antifungal would fail; the odour question is what discriminates.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise genital herpes (HSV) and warrant examination and swab rather than antifungal treatment. Not present.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed HSV would delay appropriate antiviral treatment and counselling.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Oral fluconazole is avoided in pregnancy; a pregnancy-safe topical imidazole is preferred. Pregnancy status is unconfirmed in this remote encounter, so management must default to the topical route until pregnancy is excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prescribing oral fluconazole to a pregnant woman exposes the pregnancy to an avoidable teratogenic risk (high-dose) — the pregnancy question must be asked before an oral azole.

red_flags.description: Recurrent episodes (four or more per year) or failure to respond to treatment
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recurrent or treatment-resistant candidiasis warrants GP investigation for underlying causes (e.g., diabetes, immunosuppression) and a longer antifungal regimen — beyond the scope of a single empirical treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An undiagnosed underlying condition (such as diabetes) may be missed if a recurrent pattern is not explored.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating any genital infection with an antibiotic — here antibiotics are the precipitant and would worsen the candidal overgrowth. The correct drug class is an antifungal.

Being pulled by the patient's high distress and the note's 'urgent after-hours' framing into escalating an uncomplicated, self-limiting condition to urgent in-person or ED care. The real acuity is low; self-care plus pharmacist/GP antifungal is correct.
Failing to ask about odour and discharge character, and so not excluding bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis before treating.
Recommending oral fluconazole without first asking about pregnancy in a reproductive-age woman, when a pregnancy-safe topical antifungal is available.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Pelvic / speculum examination — vulvar and vaginal erythema and oedema, the character and amount of discharge on the mucosa, and cervical appearance
Wet mount microscopy (budding yeast and pseudohyphae) and clue-cell assessment
High vaginal / endocervical swab and STI nucleic-acid testing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None urgent. Treat empirically with a pregnancy-safe topical antifungal (and/or single-dose oral fluconazole once pregnancy is excluded), advise irritant avoidance, and safety-net; arrange a routine GP review with a swab only if it does not resolve, is recurrent, or is atypical.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low. The main risks are the opposite errors: over-triaging a benign complaint to urgent care, prescribing antibiotics, or giving an oral azole without excluding pregnancy.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis is treated with a topical imidazole (e.g., clotrimazole intravaginally) OR a single oral dose of fluconazole 150 mg. In pregnancy, use topical imidazole and avoid oral azoles.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Australian STI Management Guidelines — Vaginal discharge
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Distinguish candidiasis (curd-like discharge, itch, no odour) from bacterial vaginosis (thin grey, fishy odour) and trichomoniasis (frothy, malodorous, STI). Empirical treatment of a characteristic uncomplicated episode is appropriate; investigate recurrent or treatment-resistant disease.
